normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.requirement_title: The app validates and sanitizes all untrusted inputs.
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-CODE-4 (Description): Apps have many data entry points including the UI, IPC, the network, the file system, etc. This incoming data might have been inadvertently modified by untrusted actors and may lead to bypass of critical security checks as well as classical injection attacks such as SQL injection, XSS or insecure deserialization. This control ensures that this data is treated as untrusted input and is properly verified and sanitized before it's used.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps must treat all data crossing trust boundaries (UI input, IPC, network responses, file system data, deserialized payloads) as untrusted and validate/sanitize it before use. Failure to validate enables injection attacks (SQL, XSS, command, format-string), deserialization exploits, and bypass of downstream security checks that assume well-formed input.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note: Applies universally at Tier 1+. Per Constitution Pillar 5 (Secure Architecture & Runtime), input validation discipline is universal for every Intrinsic Code app because input boundaries (UI input, IPC, network responses, file system reads, deserialization) are assumed baseline. The blueprint schema does not declare a discrete input-boundaries field; the universal applicability is enforced via the app_tier floor instead. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 1+ has at least one input boundary because UI input, network responses, or IPC payloads are baseline.

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 5 covers MASVS-CODE-4 directly via SEC-PILLAR-5-INPUT-VALIDATION: input validation and output encoding on every input boundary. This is the same control by name and substance — both require treating all data from untrusted sources as untrusted, validating against expected schema/type/range, and encoding output to prevent injection downstream. MASVS-CODE-4's scope spans every input boundary (UI, IPC, network, file system, deserialization); SEC-PILLAR-5-INPUT-VALIDATION's universal application matches that scope. Implementation correctness for specific injection-vector defenses (parameterized SQL queries, HTML output encoding, safe deserialization, format-string protection) requires per-language auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration. The mapping is partial because the discipline is universal but the implementation surface is broad (every input boundary in every file), and reaper-input is the adversarial auditor that examines actual code paths for vector-specific gaps.

expected_evidence_patterns: Input boundaries (UI text fields, network response handlers, IPC receivers, file readers, deserializers) call typed validation before use — schema validation, length bounds, type checks, allowlist matching
SQL queries use parameterized statements / prepared statements — never string concatenation with user-controlled values
JSON / XML / Protobuf deserialization uses typed parsers with schema validation; rejects unexpected fields or types
Deep link / URL scheme handlers validate the URL structure, path components, and query parameter types before dispatching
IPC receivers (Android Intent extras, iOS NSUserActivity, ContentProvider URIs) validate caller identity AND payload shape before processing
Output encoding when rendering user-controlled content: HTML-escaping for WebView display, URL-encoding for URL construction, JSON-encoding for API payloads
failure_indicators: SQL queries built via string concatenation with user-controlled values (`"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '" + userInput + "'"`) — direct SQL injection vector

Deserialization of untrusted data without schema validation (Java ObjectInputStream on attacker-controlled bytes, JSON parsing without typed schema and allowing arbitrary fields) — RCE vector via gadget chains
Deep links / URL schemes that dispatch to internal app actions without validating URL structure or path components — allows unauthorized internal operations
IPC receivers that trust caller-supplied data without payload validation — allows malicious apps to invoke internal operations with crafted payloads
WebView loading user-controlled HTML or JavaScript without output encoding — XSS vector for in-app browsers
String formatting with user-controlled format strings (printf-style format injection) — information disclosure and crash vector
File-path construction from user-controlled input without canonicalization and allowlist check (path traversal vector via `../` sequences)
false_positive_traps: Input handling inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test inputs are deterministic fixture data, not adversarial input — these are not production violations.

Input from trusted internal sources (hardcoded constants, app-bundled resources, OS-supplied data like Locale.current) is NOT 'untrusted' and doesn't require validation in the MASVS-CODE-4 sense — the violation surface is data crossing a trust boundary.

Server-side validation of inputs (when an app sends data to its own backend that validates server-side) is good defense in depth but does NOT replace client-side validation of incoming responses. MASVS-CODE-4 requires the app to validate inputs IT receives, not just inputs it sends.

References to deprecated input-handling patterns in comments, documentation, migration guides, or deprecation warnings are not violations — only their use in security-relevant code paths is a violation

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 5 input-validation discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-INPUT-VALIDATION) as primary evidence toward MASVS-CODE-4 coverage — same control by name and substance
applies input validation and output encoding at every input boundary (UI, IPC, network, file system, deserialization) per MASVS-CODE-4 universal requirement
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-CODE-4. MASVS-CODE-4 is mapped to one Constitution v0.5 trace in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-5-INPUT-VALIDATION (primary, same control by name and substance). The validation discipline is universal but the implementation surface is every input boundary in every file; reaper-input is the adversarial auditor that examines actual code paths for per-vector gaps (injection, deserialization, deep-link, IPC, network-input, file-system-input). Memory corruption vulnerabilities (mentioned in the MASVS-CODE category overview but not specifically in MASVS-CODE-4) are addressed by SEC-PILLAR-5-WX-MEMORY at Tier 4+. Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
